# **Computational Lithic Analysis: A Comprehensive Report on 3D Morphometrics, Automated Algorithms, and Digital Documentation Pipelines**

## **Introduction**

The analysis of lithic technology remains one of the foundational pillars of archaeological science, providing direct evidence of hominin cognitive evolution, raw material economy, and biomechanical behavioral adaptations. For over a century, the morphological quantification of stone tools relied heavily upon manual instrumentation, primarily utilizing digital calipers, contact goniometers, and profile gauges to extract linear and angular metrics. While these macroscopic analyses yielded critical insights into prehistoric typologies and reduction sequences, they were inherently constrained by the limitations of Euclidean geometry when applied to highly irregular, anisotropic objects. Furthermore, traditional measurement protocols are notoriously vulnerable to inter-analyst variance, subjective interpretations of anatomical reference points, and significant systematic errors, particularly when evaluating complex morphological intersections such as cortical striking platforms or acute retouched margins.  
The advent of high-resolution three-dimensional (3D) surface scanning and close-range photogrammetry has precipitated a profound epistemological and methodological shift within lithic studies. By translating physical artefacts into digital meshes—dense topological networks comprising hundreds of thousands of spatial vertices and interconnecting triangular faces—archaeologists can now bypass the limitations of physical instrumentation. This digital transition facilitates the application of advanced computational geometry, differential tensor analysis, and orientation statistics to stone tool assemblages.  
The development of open-source 3D lithic morphological analysis applications requires a rigorous synthesis of both domain-specific archaeological theory and computer vision mathematics. The algorithmic pipelines applied to artefact meshes are fundamentally designed not only to replicate classical measurement protocols with unparalleled precision but to extract entirely novel volumetric and geometric variables that were previously impossible to quantify. This report provides an exhaustive technical analysis of the state-of-the-art in computational lithic morphometrics. It details standardized metric measurement protocols, evaluates the existing ecosystem of analytical software, explores the mathematical foundations of 3D mesh edge detection and spatial orientation algorithms, delineates geometric morphometric approaches, assesses current community infrastructural needs, and outlines the rigorous photogrammetry pipelines required to generate topologically sound digital models.

## **Standardised Metric Measurement Protocols for Lithic Artefacts**

The extraction of metric data from lithic artefacts is governed by theoretical frameworks that seek to proxy the underlying fracture mechanics of stone tool production. The morphological attributes of a flake or core are direct consequences of the physical parameters applied during percussion or pressure flaking. Consequently, measurement protocols are highly standardized to ensure that variables such as blank size, utility, and reduction intensity can be compared across disparate global assemblages.

### **The Andrefsky Protocol and Linear Dimensions**

The most widely adopted traditional lithic measurement frameworks, synthesized extensively by Andrefsky and others, mandate the recording of maximum length, width, and thickness to estimate the overall utility and transport costs of lithic blanks.1 While these measurements appear intuitively simple, their exact definitions are strictly bound to anatomical reference points on the flake, which must be mathematically translated into computational algorithms.  
The extraction of maximum length—a primary proxy for blank utility and raw material constraints—is traditionally defined in two distinct ways depending on the analyst's adherence to morphological versus technological axes. Maximum length is typically measured as the greatest linear distance extending from the proximal striking platform to the distal termination, measured along a line precisely perpendicular to the striking platform width.5 Alternatively, technological length is measured strictly along the axis of flaking, which represents the trajectory of the applied force radiating from the point of percussion.6 In a computational 3D space, capturing this dimension requires the algorithm to first identify the platform plane, establish a localized Cartesian coordinate system, and compute the maximum bounding box dimension orthogonal to that proximal datum.7  
Maximum width, often referred to as technological width, is measured perpendicular to the technological length.6 Because a flake's lateral margins may converge, diverge, or remain parallel, width is frequently recorded at multiple intervals—proximal, medial, and distal—to capture the overall geometry of the blank.6 Maximum thickness is captured at the thickest point of the artefact. A critical anatomical constraint in this measurement is the explicit exclusion of the bulb of percussion; capturing the bulb would artificially inflate the thickness variable and distort calculations of the blank's overall volumetric mass.4  
Studies investigating the replicability of these standard measurements highlight significant challenges. Inter-analyst variance is particularly high when measuring platform thickness, a complexity driven by highly variable platform morphologies.4 When striking platforms are cortical, crushed, or transition smoothly into the dorsal surface without a distinct topographic ridge, human analysts struggle to consistently identify the measurement boundaries.4 Similarly, distal thickness measurements on overshot flakes (outrepassé terminations) exhibit wide variability.5 Computational models must account for these "indeterminate" topological zones by utilizing curvature-based boundary detection rather than relying on assumed sharp Euclidean intersections.

### **Striking Platform Metrics and Original Mass Estimation**

The physical dimensions of the striking platform are the most critical variables for understanding the technological control exercised by the knapper. Experimental flintknapping studies consistently demonstrate that the interaction between the striking platform and the exterior core surface dictates the geometry of the resulting flake.1  
Traditional metrics for the platform include platform width and platform thickness. Platform width is defined as the maximum transverse distance across the striking platform, from the left lateral edge to the right lateral edge.3 Platform thickness is measured perpendicular to the width, typically bisecting the point of percussion.3 While platform width has routinely been noted to correlate with overall flake size, recent research indicates that it acts more as an intermediary variable, mediating the effect of platform depth on the resulting flake width.10  
To estimate the reduction intensity of a tool—the degree to which it has been resharpened or modified from its original blank form—researchers must mathematically reconstruct the mass of the original, unmodified flake.8 Clarkson and Hiscock revolutionized this approach by introducing predictive regression models based on 3D platform area.11 Traditional macroscopic analyses estimated platform area by treating the platform surface as a simple rectangle, multiplying the linear width by the linear thickness.2 However, this gross estimation fails to capture the true irregular polygon of the platform. By utilizing 3D scanning, algorithms can precisely calculate the sum of the areas of the discrete triangular faces bounded by the platform ridge.11 This true geometric area measurement dramatically improves the predictive power of regression models, enabling highly accurate calculations of the Initial-/Terminal-Mass Comparison (ITMC) and volumetric reduction intensity.12

### **Angular Protocols: EPA, IPA, and the 3D-EdgeAngle Algorithm**

The angular relationship between the striking platform and the core surface is the primary geometric determinant in fracture mechanics. Two specific angles are quantified:

* **Exterior Platform Angle (EPA):** Measured at the intersection of the striking platform surface and the exterior (dorsal) surface of the flake or core.1 Experimental controlled studies demonstrate a highly robust positive correlation between the EPA, platform thickness, and the resulting mass and length of the flake.1  
* **Interior Platform Angle (IPA):** Measured between the striking platform and the ventral surface of the flake.18 The exact anatomical reference point for the IPA is contested; analysts must specify whether the angle is measured inclusive of the bulb of percussion, or strictly along the medial ventral plane distal to the bulb.18 The EPA and IPA generally exhibit a strong negative correlation within specific geometric ranges.19

Beyond the platform, the angle of the active retouched edge dictates the functional performance of the tool in tasks such as cutting, carving, and scraping.21 Measuring these acute margins with a contact goniometer is notoriously subjective and imprecise, frequently resulting in systematic data errors.21 To resolve this, Schunk et al. developed the 3D-EdgeAngle protocol, a script-based, semi-automated methodology engineered specifically for 3D digital meshes.21  
The 3D-EdgeAngle algorithm operates by generating virtual cross-sections strictly orthogonal to the artefact's active edge at defined intervals.21 The protocol provides three mathematical procedures for calculating the angle at specified perpendicular distances from the edge intersection (e.g., 2 mm, 5 mm, and 10 mm) 21:

| Measurement Procedure | Algorithmic Mechanism | Algorithmic Sensitivity | Reference |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **'3-points' Procedure** | Mimics a caliper by selecting the exact edge intersection vertex and two opposing face points at a strict distance (e.g., 5 mm). Calculates the angle geometrically via the resulting triangle. | Highly robust. Demonstrates the lowest mean square error, maintaining precise and consistent angular data across sections. | 21 |
| **'2-lines' Procedure** | Extracts continuous polylines along the cross-section of the dorsal and ventral faces up to the specified distance. Computes intersection of vectors from terminal points to the edge. | Highly sensitive to micro-topographical noise and material grain, resulting in higher variance. | 21 |
| **'Best-fit' Procedure** | Interpolates a linear best-fit regression through the array of vertices located along the cross-sectional profiles within the defined distance limit. | Overly sensitive to minor topographical deviations. Frequently produces misleading macroscopic angular results. | 21 |

The validation of the 3D-EdgeAngle protocol confirms that the '3-points' procedure, calculated at a distance of 10 mm from the intersection, minimizes squared error and provides the most reliable standardization for cross-assemblage comparison.21  
While international ISO standards are rarely applied directly to archaeological lithics, algorithms processing static image analyses of microscopic particles frequently comply with analogous machine-vision standards such as ISO 13322-1.26 Incorporating similar rigorous computational thresholds into 3D lithic algorithms ensures that geometric data remains inter-laboratory compatible and analytically sound.

## **Existing Software Ecosystem for 3D Lithic Analysis**

The shift toward algorithmic measurement extraction has catalyzed the development of domain-specific software applications. These programs act as translational layers, converting generalized 3D processing tasks into archaeologically relevant outputs. The primary tools populating the open-source and academic landscape currently include PyLithics, Lithics3D, and Artifact3-D (including AGMT3-D).

### **PyLithics (Python Environment)**

Developed by Gellis, Smith, and Foley, PyLithics is an open-source Python package engineered for stone tool analysis, heavily relying on computer vision libraries.27 However, PyLithics is fundamentally designed to process two-dimensional (2D) representations of lithic artefacts—specifically scanned literature illustrations, line drawings, and orthophotographs—rather than topological 3D meshes.29  
The algorithms implemented within PyLithics include pixel intensity thresholding, contour finding, and edge detection optimized for 2D matrices.29 The software successfully addresses a critical bottleneck in the discipline: capturing quantifiable size, shape, and technological data from the massive backlog of legacy illustrations.29 By transforming complex linear sequences of bases in 2D image representations into systematic data, it facilitates rapid database expansion.29 Nevertheless, its reliance on 2D input formats inherently limits its applicability to true volumetric analysis, dihedral angle computation, and 3D surface normal alignment.

### **Lithics3D (R Environment)**

Lithics3D, authored by Cornel M. Pop, is a robust, open-source R package explicitly developed for processing and analyzing 3D scans of archaeological lithics.31 The package operates simultaneously on high-level and low-level geometric paradigms, importing standard mesh formats to treat the artefacts as complex geometric objects.31  
The high-level functions of Lithics3D provide semi-automatic segmentation utilities, such as mapping global artefact thickness and automatically isolating edge angles.31 The true power of the application lies in its low-level mesh manipulation functions. It includes algorithms for calculating spatial intersections between the mesh and theoretical planes, spheres, or lines, as well as computing spherical projections, volumes, and surface areas.35  
A particularly innovative algorithm implemented in Lithics3D is its surface path routing. When an analyst needs to measure a continuous retouched edge or a non-linear dorsal ridge, they can manually place anchor landmarks. The software then connects these landmarks by executing a shortest-path algorithm (utilizing the igraph package) across the mesh vertices.35 To ensure the path follows the actual ridge rather than crossing flat surfaces, the edges of the mesh graph are weighted dynamically based on vertex curvature intensity. The weighting equation applied is ![][image1].35 This forces the algorithm to mathematically "walk" along the crest of maximal curvature, perfectly isolating continuous technological features.35

### **Artifact3-D and AGMT3-D (MATLAB Environment)**

Developed by Grosman et al. at the Computational Archaeology Laboratory of the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Artifact3-D and its geometric morphometric counterpart AGMT3-D represent the most comprehensive, GUI-driven environments for 3D lithic analysis.36 Built natively in the MATLAB Integrated Development Environment (IDE), Artifact3-D imports standard .ply, .stl, and .obj formats.7  
The software distinguishes itself through its fully automated intrinsic positioning algorithms, which align the models objectively in 3D space prior to analysis.7 Once oriented, Artifact3-D executes a suite of algorithms to compute exact volume, center of mass (CoM), surface area, asymmetry indices, and edge angles (via the integrated Angles3-D module).7 It also includes algorithms for automated flake scar identification and extraction.7  
Crucially, the measurement outputs of Artifact3-D have been rigorously validated against traditional hand-caliper measurements and precision geometric standards.23 Studies demonstrate that the digitally derived metrics are highly accurate, significantly reducing the margins of systematic and random error associated with physical instrumentation.23

| Software Application | Environment | Input Format | Primary Algorithms & Capabilities | Limitations |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **PyLithics** | Python | 2D Images, Scans | Pixel thresholding, contour finding, 2D edge detection. | Cannot process 3D meshes; limited to 2D projections. |
| **Lithics3D** | R | 3D Meshes (.ply,.stl) | igraph curvature-weighted shortest path, spatial intersections, thickness mapping. | Requires R programming proficiency; minimal GUI. |
| **Artifact3-D** | MATLAB | 3D Meshes (.ply,.stl,.obj) | Tensor PCA positioning, Center of Mass, automated scar boundary extraction, geometric morphometrics (AGMT3-D). | Requires MATLAB runtime/license; computationally intensive on large meshes. |

## **3D Mesh Edge and Flake Scar Detection Algorithms**

The automated segmentation of a lithic artefact into its archaeologically meaningful constituent parts—such as identifying the striking platform, isolating the ventral surface, mapping individual flake scars, and detecting retouched margins—poses a significant challenge in computational geometry.42 A high-resolution artefact mesh typically comprises hundreds of thousands of triangular faces.44 Detecting the intersecting ridges that divide these specific technological features requires sophisticated analysis of surface curvature.

### **Discrete Differential Geometry and the Hessian Matrix**

In classical differential geometry, the topology of a continuous surface is locally defined by its principal curvatures: ![][image2] (the maximum curvature) and ![][image3] (the minimum curvature).46 Because a 3D triangle mesh is a discrete, piecewise-linear approximation of a surface, continuous calculus cannot be applied directly.49 To resolve this, algorithms approximate localized curvature by fitting continuous quadratic patches to vertex neighborhoods, or by computing the dihedral angle—the internal angle between the normal vectors of two adjacent triangular faces.44  
By calculating the discrete Hessian matrix (![][image4]) for these localized mesh neighborhoods, algorithmic libraries can extract the principal curvatures directly as the eigenvalues of the matrix.48 The mathematical relationships are defined as:  
![][image5]  
![][image6]  
Where ![][image7] represents the Gaussian curvature and ![][image8] represents the Mean curvature.48 While widely implemented in C++ and Python libraries (such as Trimesh, Open3D, and CGAL via Laplacian smoothing operators), Gaussian and Mean curvatures possess inherent limitations for lithic analysis. Notably, bending a surface without stretching it mathematically conserves the Gaussian curvature, meaning a heavily curved cylinder may exhibit a Gaussian curvature of zero, entirely obscuring the presence of a distinct topological ridge.48

### **Shape Index, Curvedness, and Dihedral Thresholding**

To overcome the limitations of standard Gaussian curvature, computational lithic analyses heavily employ the Shape Index (![][image9]) and Curvedness (![][image10]) metrics, originally formulated by Koenderink and van Doorn.46  
The Shape Index translates the ratio of the principal curvatures into an angular polar coordinate, mapping the local geometry to a continuous, scale-independent value between ![][image11] and ![][image12] 48:  
![][image13]  
Within this framework, distinct topographical features correspond strictly to specific ![][image9] values, regardless of the object's overall size 48:

| Topological Feature | Shape Index (s) Value | Archaeological Correlate on a Lithic Mesh |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Spherical Cup** | **![][image14]** | Deepest localized depression within a flake scar (bulb negative). |
| **Rut / Valley** | **![][image15]** | Trough lines; internal scar undulations. |
| **Symmetrical Saddle** | **![][image16]** | Inflection points between intersecting scar networks. |
| **Ridge** | **![][image17]** | Scar boundaries; retouched edges; striking platform margins. |
| **Spherical Cap** | **![][image18]** | Prominent bulb of percussion; highly convex cortical surfaces. |

Curvedness (![][image10]), calculated as ![][image19], defines the absolute intensity or sharpness of that localized shape, allowing algorithms to filter out gentle undulations and isolate sharp, intentional lithic modifications.48

### **Watershed Algorithms and Scar Segmentation**

To segment individual flake scars on a core or retouched tool, algorithms must synthesize these curvature definitions to map intersecting ridge networks. Grosman et al. established a highly successful methodology by applying ridge-detection algorithms (deriving from the Yoshizawa method) to locate the boundaries of individual scars.43 However, applying raw ridge detection in isolation frequently yields false positives; algorithms detect micro-ridges caused by raw material impurities (e.g., quartz veins), mechanical micro-stepping, and digital noise.43  
Consequently, modern computational approaches synthesize curvature detection with region-growing algorithms, most notably the Watershed transform.43 The Watershed algorithm initializes by locating the local minima of the mesh topology—the deepest points or "valleys" of a flake scar where the Shape Index (![][image9]) approaches ![][image11].43 From these localized seed points, the algorithm iteratively "floods" or grows the segmented region outward until it encounters the local maxima of the boundary ridges, defined by high Curvedness (![][image10]) and a Shape Index (![][image9]) approaching ![][image20].43 This dual-stage methodology effectively isolates discrete flake scars, allowing for the automated extraction of crucial technological metrics including scar surface area, depth, mean curvature, and knapping directionality.45 These quantifiable variables are vital for reconstructing the Chaîne Opératoire and assessing core reduction intensity through continuous, rather than discrete, classification schemes.42

## **Platform Orientation Algorithms and Spatial Alignment**

Before meaningful comparative metrics can be algorithmically extracted, a 3D mesh must be standardly oriented within a Cartesian coordinate system. Manual alignment in virtual space is highly subjective, introducing severe rotational and translational variance that degrades downstream analyses. Archaeologists define the standard orientation for lithic flakes as aligning the striking platform horizontally (orthogonal to the Z-axis) and precisely centering the point of percussion.52 For cores, orientation typically aligns the primary flaking surface or striking platform along equivalent standardized planes.7

### **Surface Normal and Tensor Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**

Automated positioning relies upon Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied directly to the geometric properties of the mesh. Software solutions such as Artifact3-D implement objective positioning algorithms utilizing surface normal analysis.7  
The primary Normal Positioning method utilizes the normal vector and the surface area of every individual triangular face comprising the topological mesh. The algorithm calculates a surface tensor covariance matrix.7 From this matrix, the eigenvectors are extracted; these eigenvectors mathematically reflect three orthogonal planes that minimize the object's morphological asymmetry under reflection.7 The artefact mesh is then rotated in virtual space so that these calculated eigenvectors correspond exactly to the X, Y, and Z axes of a common Euclidean space.7  
Alternatively, Inertia Positioning algorithms compute the center of mass (CoM) assuming homogeneous material density, utilizing the radial vectors extending from the origin to each vertex.7 The principal axes of inertia are computed, and the object is oriented such that its longest geometric dimension aligns with the Y-axis, its width aligns with the X-axis, and its thickness aligns with the Z-axis.7

### **Automated Platform Segmentation**

Once the mesh is spatially aligned, the automated detection and segmentation of the striking platform surface itself requires searching the mesh topology for specific signature boundaries. A striking platform on a flake is generally characterized by a relatively planar, faceted, or gently convex geometric region bounded abruptly by high-curvature ridges—specifically, the Exterior Platform Angle (EPA) boundary on the dorsal side and the Interior Platform Angle (IPA) boundary situated above the bulb of percussion.1  
Algorithms isolate the platform by traversing the proximal extent of the properly oriented mesh, executing dihedral angle thresholding to identify the abrupt, localized angular deviations that define the platform edge.43 The bounded planar region is then segmented for discrete platform area calculation. By fully automating this spatial alignment and segmentation process, computational tools can sequentially and continuously evaluate massive assemblages without manual intervention.52 This high-throughput capability is an absolute prerequisite for generating the vast, standardized training datasets necessary to power emergent Machine Learning virtual knapping models and predictive neural networks.52

## **Geometric Morphometrics (GM) for Lithic Shape Analysis**

While linear measurements distill a complex artefact into a highly simplified geometric bounding box, Geometric Morphometrics (GM) captures the true global morphology of a tool, mathematically analyzing its shape independent of its absolute size, spatial position, and rotational orientation. GM approaches have seen rapid adoption and methodological refinement in lithic analysis to study allometry, morphological integration, raw material constraints, and typo-technological variation across spatiotemporal gradients.55

### **Landmark and Semi-Landmark Schemes**

The foundation of GM requires the precise placement of homologous coordinate points (landmarks) across a sample population. In biological anthropology, landmarks strictly correlate to specific biological and developmental anatomical junctions.55 Because lithic artefacts are reductive, abiotic objects that lack true biological homology, archaeologists apply the concept of "geometric homology" by positioning landmarks at points of technological or functional significance.55  
Common landmark configurations are specifically tailored by tool type:

* **Bifaces and Handaxes:** The complex, continuous curves of Acheulean handaxes and bifacial tools are typically captured utilizing 2D or 3D outline-based schemes.36 Analysts place fixed anchor landmarks at the extreme proximal base and distal tip. Subsequently, an equidistant array of sliding semi-landmarks (frequently 60 or more points) is strung along the continuous lateral margins.57 These semi-landmarks are mathematically permitted to "slide" along the outline trajectory during Procrustes superimposition to minimize bending energy, ensuring they perfectly capture the non-linear edge profile without creating false localized variance.57  
* **Blades, Flakes, and Projectile Points:** Landmark schemes for flakes generally anchor critical technological junctions: the point of percussion, the extreme lateral extremities of the striking platform, the points of maximum lateral expansion, and the distal termination point.41 Standardized 13-point and 16-point 3D landmark configurations are frequently published for specialized typologies such as Clovis points or Levallois blanks.60 For comprehensive volumetric analysis, complex 3D configurations may also include a dense grid of surface semi-landmarks draped mathematically over the dorsal and ventral convexities.41

### **The Analytical Software Ecosystem: Geomorph and MorphoJ**

Once landmark coordinates are acquired, they must undergo Generalized Procrustes Analysis (GPA).40 GPA mathematically translates all specimen configurations to a common spatial origin, scales them to a uniform centroid size, and iteratively rotates them to minimize the sum of squared Euclidean distances between corresponding landmarks.40 The resulting coordinates—Procrustes residuals—reflect pure geometric shape variation independent of size.  
The statistical exploration of this shape space is overwhelmingly conducted in two primary software environments:

1. **MorphoJ:** Authored by Klingenberg, MorphoJ is a standalone GUI application that imports raw or Procrustes-aligned coordinate data to perform Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Discriminant Function Analysis (DFA), and Canonical Variate Analysis (CVA).60 MorphoJ provides powerful visual outputs, such as wireframe graphs and thin-plate spline deformation grids. These grids warp a hypothetical "mean shape" into the extremes of the principal components, visually demonstrating how variables like reduction intensity influence specific geometries, such as point base concavity or blade edge convergence.62  
2. **Geomorph (R Package):** Operating natively within the R programming environment, geomorph provides highly advanced, scriptable functions for digitizing 2D and 3D landmarks, executing Procrustes superimposition (including optimizing sliding semi-landmarks), and conducting sophisticated multivariate parametric and non-parametric statistics.64

To circumvent the tedious, time-consuming manual placement of dense 3D landmarks, automated protocols such as the *StyroStone* method have been proposed.58 The StyroStone methodology, integrated with software like AGMT3-D, allows for the fully automated fitting and alignment of 3D semi-landmark grids over dense meshes, radically increasing throughput for analyzing massive, small-tool assemblages (e.g., microliths and bladelets).41

## **Photogrammetry and Scanning Pipelines for Lithic Documentation**

The mathematical efficacy of algorithmic edge detection, surface normal calculation, and geometric morphometrics strictly depends upon the topological fidelity of the input 3D mesh.41 Generating digital models free from geometric noise, topological holes, and textural optical distortions requires rigorous data acquisition pipelines.69 While high-end structured-light scanners (e.g., Artec Micro, Revopoint) and Micro-Computed Tomography (Micro-CT) provide exceptional sub-millimeter accuracy and volumetric closure 41, Close-Range Photogrammetry (Structure from Motion, SfM) remains the most ubiquitous, scalable, and cost-effective methodology for widespread archaeological deployment.11

### **Optimal Camera Settings and Optical Physics**

To successfully capture the micro-topography necessary for accurate scar segmentation and dihedral angle measurement, high-resolution DSLR or mirrorless camera sensors paired with dedicated macro lenses (e.g., 60mm to 100mm focal lengths) are mandatory.74  
The highly acute edges of lithic artefacts present severe optical depth-of-field challenges.74 To maintain the entire artefact edge in sharp focus, a photographer might normally utilize an extremely narrow aperture (e.g., \> f/16). However, doing so introduces diffraction-limited blurring, which destroys the sharp micro-textures required by photogrammetry algorithms. To circumvent this physics limitation, analysts must employ z-stacking (focus stacking).75 This process involves capturing a sequence of images at varying focal planes across the depth of the artefact and utilizing software to blend the perfectly in-focus regions pixel-by-pixel, generating a single image with infinite apparent depth of field.75  
The physical distance between the camera sensor and the artefact is dictated by the minimum focus distance of the macro lens (e.g., approximately 32 cm for a standard 60mm macro lens), establishing the absolute resolution limit of the native images.74 Furthermore, absolute spatial scale must be rigorously established within the frame, typically utilizing calibrated millimeter paper, grid mats, or standardized scale bars that photogrammetry processing software can automatically detect and align.72

### **Turntable Configurations and Environmental Radiometry**

Capturing small, geometrically complex artefacts necessitates highly controlled, stable macro environments. A standardized, portable photogrammetry rig typically consists of a non-reflective black velvet backdrop, interlocking foam stabilization tiles, artificial LED lighting, and a manual or automated motorized turntable (such as a lazy susan).41 The artefact is temporarily secured to the turntable utilizing non-residue modeling clay, allowing for complete 360-degree orbital capture.41  
A critical and frequently overlooked consideration in lithic photogrammetry is the inherent radiometry of the raw material. Materials such as obsidian, crystalline quartz, and specific high-quality chalcedonies exhibit high specular reflectivity and subsurface scattering (translucency).41 These optical properties fundamentally break the feature-matching algorithms (SIFT/SURF) inherent to SfM software like Agisoft Metashape, COLMAP, and OpenDroneMap.41  
To mitigate these fatal reconstruction errors, an aggressive cross-polarization setup must be deployed.73 By placing a linear polarizing film directly over the artificial light sources (e.g., LED arrays or macro ring flashes) and attaching a perpendicular circular polarizing filter to the camera lens, all direct specular highlights and internal glaring are optically eliminated. The camera sensor subsequently receives only diffuse, Lambertian reflections, preserving the distinct, matte micro-textural details needed for algorithm convergence and successful mesh generation.73 For highly reflective assemblages, alternative lighting techniques such as Reflectance Transformation Imaging (RTI) are also heavily utilized to capture and enhance surface microtopography.74

### **Photogrammetric vs. Caliper Accuracy**

When acquisition protocols are rigorously adhered to, the achievable accuracy of photogrammetric models routinely rivals that of expensive structured light scanners. Validation studies demonstrate that dimensional variables (maximum length, width, thickness) extracted from high-quality photogrammetric meshes exhibit near-zero mean deviation when compared to digital caliper measurements, significantly outperforming human inter-analyst replicability.5 The automated extraction of data from photogrammetry models removes the subjective placement of caliper jaws on complex curves.23 However, it must be noted that standard photogrammetry continues to struggle with extremely thin, highly translucent edges (e.g., acute bladelet margins), where Micro-CT currently provides superior topological closure and volumetric accuracy.41

## **Community Needs and the Digital Trajectory**

The rapid expansion of the digital lithic toolset has generated a vibrant ecosystem of open-source scripts, analytical libraries, and methodological frameworks. This community-driven infrastructure is centrally indexed by specialized repositories such as open-archaeo.info, which categorizes hundreds of scripts explicitly targeting artefact morphology, 3D modeling, and statistical spatial analysis.33 Analysis of digital archaeology forums, GitHub repositories, and platform registries reveals distinct community priorities and pain points regarding the current state of digital infrastructure.79

### **The Demand for Automation and Transparent Reproducibility**

Lithic analysts overwhelmingly desire tools capable of high-throughput, automated extraction of functionally and technologically relevant variables to process massive legacy assemblages.13 Specific highly sought-after capabilities include scripts for automated 3D morphometric slicing (exemplified by tools like lithic3dslicer by kotdijian) 77, algorithms for extracting objective edge angles (like the 3D\_EdgeAngle package by Schunk et al.) 82, and reliable regression models capable of generating standard reduction indices to reconstruct discarded blank utility.17  
Crucially, there is a distinct community pivot away from "black-box" proprietary software toward open-source, mathematically transparent environments like R and Python. Researchers demand the ability to peer into the underlying algorithmic logic—such as auditing exactly how an interior platform angle is mathematically derived from a mesh normal—to ensure epistemological validity and absolute reproducibility in their published datasets.19

### **Systemic Challenges and AI Technical Barriers**

Despite immense enthusiasm, the widespread deployment of computational methods is currently hindered by software dependency management and code obsolescence. Discussions within archaeological computing spheres highlight acute frustrations with rapidly outdated software versions, conflicting package environments, and broken dependencies in legacy scripts.79  
Attempting to bridge these coding gaps via Large Language Models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT to generate ad-hoc Python or R scripts has proven highly hazardous.79 Community feedback indicates that LLMs frequently hallucinate outdated syntax, invent non-existent analytical modules, and—most critically—lack the fundamental domain logic necessary to properly parameterize fracture mechanics or differential geometry for lithic surfaces.79 Analysts report that LLMs default to suggesting procedures based on the frequency of generic data science documentation rather than archaeological accuracy, resulting in a dangerous mishmash of directions.79 Consequently, the field requires robustly maintained, containerized software deployments and rigorously curated libraries that guarantee long-term operational stability and domain-specific accuracy, rather than relying on generalized AI coding assistants.

## **Conclusion**

The integration of high-fidelity 3D scanning, sophisticated mesh processing algorithms, and robust open-source software architectures has fundamentally transformed the discipline of lithic morphometrics. The transition from simplistic linear caliper measurements to dynamic, global topological analyses ensures a previously unattainable level of objectivity and cross-assemblage reproducibility.  
Protocols like Andrefsky’s linear models and Dibble’s platform angle assessments are now being mathematically formalized through continuous tensor and curvature algorithms. Innovations such as the 3D-EdgeAngle protocol standardize the measurement of critical functional attributes by eliminating manual goniometric error, relying instead on precise multi-point mesh intersections that drastically reduce mean square error. Similarly, the application of complex discrete differential geometry—leveraging Gaussian curvature, shape indices, and the discrete Hessian matrix—allows specialized software like Lithics3D and Artifact3-D to seamlessly isolate continuous retouched ridges and automatically segment individual flake scars. The integration of geometric morphometrics through tools like geomorph and MorphoJ further expands analytical capabilities, capturing the entire geometrical variance of an artefact independently of simple size scaling.  
However, the mathematical efficacy of this computational ecosystem remains intimately bound to the rigorous execution of digital documentation. The deployment of stringent close-range photogrammetry pipelines—incorporating critical techniques such as focus stacking, cross-polarization to defeat specular reflectivity, and standardized turntable orientations—is mandatory to provide the algorithms with topographically sound data. Moving forward, as open-source libraries continue to standardize extraction metrics and community demands push for greater transparency and reproducibility, the fusion of 3D morphometrics and automated computational segmentation will not merely complement traditional macroscopic lithic analysis, but replace it as the definitive standard for interpreting past human technological behavior.

#### **Works cited**

1. The Lithic Assemblages of Qafzeh Cave \- National Academic Digital Library of Ethiopia, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://www.ndl.ethernet.edu.et/bitstream/123456789/21203/1/118.pdf](https://www.ndl.ethernet.edu.et/bitstream/123456789/21203/1/118.pdf)

78. zackbatist/open-archaeo: A list of open source archaeological software and resources \- GitHub, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://github.com/zackbatist/open-archaeo](https://github.com/zackbatist/open-archaeo)  
79. AI and Archaeology \- Reddit, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://www.reddit.com/r/Archaeology/comments/18nwa2f/ai\_and\_archaeology/](https://www.reddit.com/r/Archaeology/comments/18nwa2f/ai_and_archaeology/)  
80. Artefact morphology \- open-archaeo, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://open-archaeo.info/tags/artefact-morphology/](https://open-archaeo.info/tags/artefact-morphology/)  
81. open-archaeo.csv \- GitHub, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://github.com/zackbatist/open-archaeo/blob/master/open-archaeo.csv](https://github.com/zackbatist/open-archaeo/blob/master/open-archaeo.csv)  
82. 3D\_EdgeAngle \- open-archaeo, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://open-archaeo.info/post/3d-edgeangle/](https://open-archaeo.info/post/3d-edgeangle/)  
83. 3D modelling \- open-archaeo, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://open-archaeo.info/tags/3d-modelling/](https://open-archaeo.info/tags/3d-modelling/)  
84. GitHub \- lschunk/3D\_EdgeAngle: The data within this repository is related to 3D-EdgeAngle, a semi-automated 3D digital method to systematically quantify stone tool edge angle and design., accessed May 23, 2026, [https://github.com/lschunk/3D\_EdgeAngle](https://github.com/lschunk/3D_EdgeAngle)  
85. Retouch (lithics) \- Wikipedia, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retouch\_(lithics)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retouch_\(lithics\))
